item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find the concept of each side effecting a different area pretty interesting. I just wish all 6 sides were explored and not just 2.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two hours later, a research assistant returned from the break room with news that a tsunami had occurred in the Indian Ocean and caused hundreds of thousands of deaths and extensive property damage.

Seeing this ancient trope out in the wild made me chuckle a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would suggest a rewrite from the author itself or any other, including a proper writing in the experiments' logs as other users suggested. Mainly because they lack the scientific "coldness" and professionalism the foundation has.

The story doesn't hold up as to why in the first place it is an SCP, since it was just an overly complicated rubik's cube when the foundation found it in the cathedral (btw innecesary deaths were administrated. Amnestics or mind-control is usually used in this case. Or relocation as class D personnel).

If they knew somehow it was an SCP by writings or any other source, they must have known its effects. Nevertheless, the first experiment is not an experiment by itself. Just recording the shifts of the box does not count as one, they ought to be analyzing something. Then, the second experiment is not well-thought knowing they just discovered in that instance that the object was an SCP, and the correlation of the events is totally random. They have thousands of SCP and many of them are capable of causing a tsunami.

Document 90-B should be included as to why they tried to destroy the box in the first place rather than searching for the mechanism doing the shifts. Also, why would they try to destroy something that might have been reclassified as keter? Didn't they know before that it could provoke XK-class disasters?

The experiment 0240 should be rewritten since the core of the story is located there. The cube is sentient, it self-defends itself if someone tries to shift it. But it doesn't feel that way.

And I feel the safety measures should be rewritten too, since they do not seem feasable as to capture a picture of an entire 100x100 grid with a class D personnel (and not stationary cameras that automatically send pictures with every shift to the core computer for analysis). Also, if any experiment log records that the box can be stopped, the object should be

And the class of the object seems a bit odd. If the object is stoppable from its shifts without causing any harm is a safe object. If not, it's euclid. But no special measures are used to stop it, for it to be considered keter class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey dude, check the date on that post. It's like four years old. Though by the looks of your comment you may have hit the wrong button.

item.title: Full item completion has been hypothesized to cause an unparalleled disaster to occur.
item.content:encoded:
"Full item completion has been hypothesized to cause an unparalleled disaster to occur."

wait what? There's no basis for that.
Full item completion will probably make six pretty pictures, i see no reason to believe anything more would happen

item.title: Mod Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Raylord1298, you've already been told not to roleplay in the forums, as per site behavioral expectations. Please post in discussion threads with content that indicates you are aware that the Foundation is a fictional entity and that you are speaking out-of-character.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
welp, just to tell you all, if all sides are aligned, then your dimension is screwed.
.
.
.
.
.
p.s., your dimension is the only one to have a "supercube"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would it be alright to change one line of this? I want to change "unparallelled disaster" to "K-Class scenario."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The dumber elements do keep me from giving this an upvote (such as it being sentient), but there's plenty of good here. While the concept is nothing super original, I always enjoy this "something would cause certain doom for the world… if it wasn't so slow we'll long be gone by the time it gets to that".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get why you take issue with the Tsunami. The researcher’s are just covering their bases. For gods sake man, they work with things that could end the world, who knows what is linked to what. Other then that, I get where you are coming from, and can understand your issues.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, and have upvoted, but it could use some polishing. Is the original author still around?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, I would've liked this a lot more if it was just a quaint little cube that moved its pieces around a lot with no anomalous properties at all. Then the Foundation, being the paranoid organization it is, began to draw conclusions and parallels between the cube and natural disasters, believing it to be some sort of doomsday instigator/countdown and get super scared of it before realizing it's just a really nifty Rubik's cube.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The item itself seems like a great concept, part mysterious but also leaving clues to it's own function.

But, how would you keep watch of 60.000, that's sixty-thousand, cubes of the size 2mm X 2mm X 2mm ?

And how did the Foundation get tipped off about it?
Was it a coveted object of said church? Did it "change" someone's life? Why would this object warrant the killing of 6 monks, a priest and the church being burned down?

And how, if I may ask, did nobody know the significance of this object, and still agree to seize it from the church?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Having read, and re-read the article I had to downvote for the retrieval method (we burnt down a cathedral and killed seven people), as well as the logs - people have mentioned the "no correlation found between this and events we wouldn't connect it with anyway" several times already.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So… it is Cube of Doom (TM). nope.png

item.title: Re: possible rewrite?
item.content:encoded:
Dude, it's +50 ish. Doubt a rewrite will happen any time soon unless the old author returns and decides to do it.

I still love this concept and how it's handled, personally.

item.title: possible rewrite?
item.content:encoded: Good idea, poor execution. This needs a rewrite and I beleive the original author isn't around anymore. Now the only question is who to write it and the question of mod permission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually like this one, but there's one thing bugging me:
If it's 100x100, you can't define specific sides because there are no fixed center pieces. Perhaps make it 101x101?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
CUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUBE

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How do you spaghettiing keep records of a cube with 60,000 points, of which 400 change every 2.8 seconds? I mean… visualize a 2mm x 2mm square. Any design on it would have to be miniscule. This SCP is not logistically plausible.

Plus, the whole disaster/accident predicting thing is kind of lame in the first place.

E: This isn't Keter, or even particularly dangerous if you leave it alone. And you don't need to terminate non-AXA personnel in the facility, because geez. It also raises the question of "why is there special terminology just for this one SCP"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The tone is fine. The experiment log is written in a more colloquial manner than the rest, but that's because it's a log rather than a refined document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why the hell is this still here? The tone is awful, the execution of this idea is cliche, and the containment procedure are strange.
I do like the idea of Rubik's cube sort of object slowly solving itself, but the fact that it 'causes' real world disasters isn't helping this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dig the main idea, but I have one huge irk with it. Why do they have records going back a few hundred years if they have no idea as to its location before residing at the cathedral? Or is it supposed to be that it was at said cathedral for said hundreds of years?

Either way, +1, cool idea with (mostly) good execution, and I'm all about a good execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…

Yeah, that was entirely unintentional. I could probably start a pun war, but I believe I shall refrain. :-P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I guess (remembers it's Drewbear, and double checks that it's not a mod-post)
I guess I should be glad I was randomly going through SCPs then. I think that It could simply be reordered, moving the circumstances of acquisition to its own section. Also, I believe that the tsunami can easily be substituted for another, less publicized, natural disaster, like a tornado hitting near the facility.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Indian Ocean Tsunami

wave of SCPs

*snerks*

No, but seriously, I like this one's basic idea, it just needs a major cleanup. Is the original author around, still?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The handling of the Indian tsunami is somewhat forgivable, since this was written before that particular cliche was identified and denounced. In fact, this was part of the wave of SCPs that prompted that cliche. I think it's the only one that's survived, as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. This is bad. Like really bad. Retrieval before physical description, very awkward tone, extremely poor redactions, extremely poor handling of actual real-world catastrophe, the works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In my scouring of the lists and reading of random SCPs, I came across this. Shouldn't the object class say "Safe Euclid Keter"?
E: Also, Does it say somewhere that it could hover? Because I can imagine it gradually moving via the sides turning, thus it shuffles along the floor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting, though there are quite a few things off, which other people have stated. I would also like to put my vote in for a picture. An article like this deserves it, I'll look and see if I can find one, and I may be able to create a 3d model.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An old SCP, yes, but it doesn't seem to get the attention it deserves. I like it, as it was one of the first articles I read.

I don't believe there is a tale written about this one yet. I would love to see what people think will happen upon completion of the puzzle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mayhaps it's a new addition, but the complaints of "how do they know to look for a correlation" seem unfounded when you consider that records of cube's alignments have existed for nearly 500 years.

It would not take a genius to analyse these logs and historical events to note a correlation between multiple-string segments and adverse events.

Overall, I quite like this. It could use a little TLC, but the improvements I'd recommend are mostly formatting.

item.title: Re: Possible image
item.content:encoded:
It's fair if you just think it looks like crap in general, but that's not the same as failing to match the physical description.

For the record, the "triangles" were not shopped at all; the whole thing is an actual still of a digitally-rendered 100 x 100 Rubik's Cube-type model in mid-rotation. The only photoshopping I did was to alter the colour and texture to try and capture the "scrambled white design" factor. I went for the blurry, unclear quality for effect(like many SCP images on the site), hence the caption acknowledging it. Real life infrared imaging has the same grainy, CGI-looking effect.(although I guess if you have to explain that to the reader, their gut reaction is going to be diminished).

Anyway, it's fair if you just think it looks bad, but I definitely disagree about it being inaccurate or the blur factor ruining it. At any rate, this is gonna be a hard one to do justice, because it implies such a great visual.

item.title: Re: Possible image
item.content:encoded: Because it looks like a blurry cube with poorly shooped triangles on it.

item.title: Re: Possible image
item.content:encoded: tbh, it looks like a bunch of really rough iron packed together.

item.title: Re: Possible image
item.content:encoded: In what ways does it not match the description of a black Rubik's-style cube with ten thousand two-millimeter segments on each side, speckled with the tiny fragments of a white design split up all over them, which shifts one thin column at a time?

item.title: Re: Possible image
item.content:encoded:
It's obviously photoshopped.

Plus, it looks awful while somehow managing to not match the description.

item.title: Re: Possible image
item.content:encoded: I don't think that matches the description.

item.title: Possible image
item.content:encoded: Sorry about the mixup earlier. Here's the image I created: "Low-res still from infrared footage of SCP-090 during a standard single-column realignment event".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't add pictures to articles without author or staff permission. Page has been reverted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know what iteration this currently is or just how rewritten it's been, but as it is right now, I think it's excellent. The logs toward the end are chilling - I almost felt my stomach lurch when it suddenly did the ten-segment alignment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely, the experiment logs seem to suggest they suspect a correlation between natural disasters and the object's actions before having seen any reason to believe so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It made me wonder, is the movement causing catastrophes? Or are the catastrophes causing the movement? The article seems to imply that every time segments line up, bad things happen, but there's nothing to say which is causing which. I got the impression that this thing is just displaying, in some rather strange way, the "state of reality" or some such. Think of a memory monitor displaying the state of a running computer program…

The reaction when people tried to manually move it even may not have been the object acting upon its own accord, but simply being unable to be manually moved, and simultaneously updating to display some feedback that occurred due to the attempt. Or pure coincidence…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept, however the Experiments could use some editing. Such as the fact that they technically aren't experiments (except when they have a D-class go and shift it) and the fact that in the first "experiment" they say "No correlation between shifts and any world events found yet." and then in the second say "Two hours later, a research assistant returned from the break room with news that a tsunami had occurred … No correlation is currently known, but we will make note of it."

They seem to know that there is a correlation before they have sufficient proof, but at first they deny that they know of a correlation. I think it would be better to note in the description that the alignments seem to predict negative events as opposed to trying to make a log out of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to dodge the whole old version vs new version discussion (since I never read the new version in the first place) and just say that I love the concept of a puzzle trying to solve itself. Although it may not be, since it was able to create an alignment at will when interfered with.

Actually, this may work better if it's not sentient. Imagine a puzzle counting down to the endtimes as its mechanism slowly solves it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But to say the old one was better than the new one? That's adding insult to injury.

The old one is much better simply because it didn't redact half the page. Plus, you wrote the new version in a drastically less scientific tone. With way too many words. And unrealistic, unnecessary dialogue (no dialogue appears at all in the old version).

There are some egregiously dumb things in the old version (Indian ocean tsunami… sigh), but overall it gets the concept across better simply by being shorter and cleaner.

It may be true that you are now a better writer, but I find it strange that you never even asked why everyone thinks the old version is better. Very few writers improve by ignoring all feedback.

EDIT: Still downvoting this version for the egregiously dumb shit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry you feel that way, but people obviously did feel that the new version was not an improvement. Even if you don't like the writing quality, the article is still well-regarded. Feel free to leave and not come back, if that's what you so choose, but you can always turn those efforts to a new SCP instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Look, I'm going to come right out and say it, as the author of both versions of the article, the old one was not good. If people don't like the new one, that's one thing. But to say the old one was better than the new one? That's adding insult to injury. I could live with the page just being deleted, but I'm not going to stick around a site with my ancient schlock flapping around in the breeze.

I thank you for considering my rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Page reverted as per agreement with bright.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wasn't this thing sitting at zero just yesterday? How'd it get so much higher without anyone commenting favorably on the new version?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This new version is confusing enough to earn my downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kinda liked the old version. But this new one is a muddled mess. It's unclear what it does, there's too much expunged, and the formatting and writing are poor. Plus, all the black boxes are the same length. That seems very odd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I admit I don't recall what the old version was like, but the new version feels like a chaotic and uninteresting mess (and doesn't look like a Keter to me.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry to say that, but current version of the object is really worse than it was earlier. Too much unnecessary details, odd calculations and "████████". And, of course, some moments in text made me cry:

We ask that future team members with access to the chamber keep their hands clear of SCP-090.

I imagine: new team members suddenly break into 090 contaiment cell after the midnight just to grope or play with a Keter class object. How unprofessional. If that's so, they're kids, not Foundation staff.

████████ was eliminated and his/her assets seized by the facility at the time of acquisition.

Guess, "seized by Foundation"

"The request for a predictive supercomputer (████████) has been placed on hold indefinitely, so all data parsing is currently under jurisdiction of ████████ personal computer"

My 343, Foundation is too slow (or too greedy), to provide a supercomputer for a Keter. Sounds like 090 is not a Keter and just causes uncontrollable flatulence in exposed personnel with every object's realignment.

Downvoted, until all this weak places will be fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"The conclusions drawn bear no relation to the events observed."

I agree. There seems to be very little science being applied to this SCP. If its effects weren't so overwhelmingly dramatic, then you could get some experimentation done with it. SCPs are always more interesting when experiments can be run on them. 682 is one of the deadliest things under Foundation containment but they experiment on it all the time!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, it's not amazing, and it's poorly written in places.

Experiment 0012: Observation is going well, we have managed to develop a system to record and analyze the shifts in the cube almost as quickly as they occur. No correlation between shifts and any world events found yet.

First, no experiment has been performed here. The bigger problem, though, is that it's written in the present tense, so I assume it's written as a direct report immediately after the 'experiment', and therefore the last sentence makes no sense whatsoever. Why is the researcher commenting on the connection between the cube and 'world events' if no connection has yet been discovered?

Experiment 0048: We observed a six segment alignment today on the 1st side. It was noted and passed without incident. Two hours later, a research assistant returned from the break room with news that a tsunami had occurred in the Indian Ocean and caused hundreds of thousands of deaths and extensive property damage. No correlation is currently known, but we will make note of it.

Here, as well. What prompted the researcher to connect those two events? If 'no correlation is currently known', then why is it being mentioned? My microwave broke down on the day of the Indian tsunami, I didn't note that connection in my diary. The researcher is basically saying "we have not yet discovered that these two events are linked, but we will discover that in the future."

Experiment 0172: A six segment alignment was recorded this morning on the 'local' side. As a safety precaution, Site ██ was evacuated. Two hours later, a containment breach occurred, but resulted in no loss of life due to the evacuation. Object determined to predict events, not cause them. 1st side designated as 'global'. Upgrade to Euclid status requested.

I don't understand. The conclusions drawn bear no relation to the events observed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article is actually kinda meh.

item.title: Containment
item.content:encoded: drop it into a metal box only big enough for it to fit, and not big enough for it to turn. hm hm hm….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. Would it be possible to get a picture?
